nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found from the surface to about 20 m depth in coral-rich lagoon reefs and shallow channels with high current; occasionally on seaward reefs

decorPreferences: live rock with numerous hiding places
areas of moderate to strong flow

diet.notes: An opportunistic omnivore. In the wild, has been documented feeding on microscopic algae, plankton, and small sessile invertebrates, and is known to prey on pest anemones such as Aiptasia and Majano. In captivity, generally considered to readily accept prepared foods including meaty items (mysid shrimp, chopped marine seafoods, frozen preparations) supplemented with marine algae and vegetable matter. Small frequent feedings are generally advised, especially during acclimation.

summary: An Indo-Pacific butterflyfish identified by two dark saddle-like patches on a white body with fine vertical lines and a yellow rear. Generally considered one of the hardier Chaetodon species, but typically regarded as not reef-safe; best suited to large fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) systems.
careNotes:
The Double Saddle Butterflyfish (Chaetodon ulietensis) is generally considered one of the hardier members of its genus and is commonly reported to make the transition to aquarium life more readily than many other Chaetodon species. It tends to accept prepared and frozen foods well, which contributes to its reputation as an intermediate-level rather than advanced species.

Reef-safety is nuanced for this fish. Aquarium trade sources consistently flag it as not reef-safe, citing the potential to prey on mushroom corals, anemones, stony corals, and soft corals. At the same time, reef-keeper accounts note that individuals rarely feed on ornamental corals in practice and can be useful for controlling pest anemones such as Aiptasia and Majano — small hitchhiker anemones that can overrun a reef tank if left unchecked. The cautious approach is to assume the fish will sample corals and house it in a fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) system, though some experienced keepers have maintained individuals alongside corals with mixed results.

In the wild, this species is generally encountered singly or in pairs and is believed to form monogamous bonds. When keeping a pair, both fish should be introduced simultaneously to avoid territorial tension. The Double Saddle Butterflyfish is not territorial and ranges widely across the reef while grazing, so a long tank footprint and open swimming space matter more than absolute volume — a 150 cm or longer tank is generally advised.
